items.question: How long does an infrastructure penetration test take?
items.answer: Typically 1-3 weeks, depending on the size and complexity of your infrastructure.

items.question: Do we need to give you VPN access?
items.answer: VPN access is required for internal testing.For external testing, only publicly accessible assets are tested.

items.question: Will testing disrupt our operations?
items.answer: We use non-intrusive testing methods during business hours and coordinate all invasive testing.

items.question: What happens if you find critical vulnerabilities?
items.answer: We will immediately notify your security team and provide remediation instructions.

items.question: Are you also testing our cloud infrastructure?
items.answer: Yes, we test both on-premise and cloud infrastructure including AWS, Azure and Google Cloud.

items.question: What access data do you need from us?
items.answer: Credentials vary depending on the test type - from no credentials for black box testing to domain admin for comprehensive testing.

introduction.title: introduction
introduction.content: At sodusecure we are committed to protecting your privacy.This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, disclose and protect your information when you visit our website.

collect.title: Information We Collect
collect.intro: We may collect information about you in various ways.The information we may collect on the website includes:

personalItems.name: Name, email address, telephone number and contact information
personalItems.account: Account login details and profile information

usageItems.pages: Pages visited and time spent on pages

use.title: How we use your information
use.intro: We use the information we collect in the following ways:

items.provide: To provide, operate and maintain our Services
items.process: To process transactions and send related information
items.admin: To manage your account and provide customer support
items.respond: To respond to your inquiries and fulfill your requirements
items.monitor: To monitor and analyze usage patterns and trends
items.detect: To detect, prevent and resolve technical issues and security threats
items.marketing: To send you marketing and promotional communications (with your consent)
items.comply: To comply with legal obligations and enforce our Terms

sharing.title: Sharing your information
sharing.intro: We may share your information in the following situations:
sharing.providersDesc: We share information with third-party service providers who perform services on our behalf
sharing.transfersDesc: We may share or transfer your information in connection with a merger, acquisition or sale of assets
sharing.legalDesc: We may disclose your information if required to do so by law or in response to valid government requests
sharing.consentDesc: We may share your information for any other purpose with your consent
sharing.notSell: We do not sell, trade or rent your personal information to third parties for marketing purposes.

security.title: Security of your information
security.content: We use administrative, technical and physical security measures to protect your personal information.However, no method of transmission over the Internet or electronic storage is 100% secure and we cannot guarantee its absolute security.

rights.title: Your privacy rights
rights.intro: Depending on your location, you may have the following rights regarding your personal information:
rights.accessDesc: Request access to your personal information
rights.correctionDesc: Request to correct inaccurate or incomplete information
rights.deletionDesc: Request deletion of your personal information
rights.portabilityDesc: Request a copy of your information in a portable format
rights.optOutDesc: Objection to marketing communication and data processing
rights.withdrawDesc: Revoke your consent to data processing at any time
rights.contact: To exercise any of these rights, please contact us using the information provided below.

cookies.title: Cookies and tracking technologies
cookies.intro: We use cookies and similar tracking technologies to track activity on our Service and store certain information.Types of cookies we use:
cookies.essentialDesc: Required for the website to function properly
cookies.performanceDesc: Help us understand how visitors interact with our website
cookies.marketingDesc: Used to deliver relevant advertising to you
cookies.browser: You can instruct your browser to refuse all cookies or to indicate when a cookie is being sent.However, if you do not accept cookies, you may not be able to use some parts of our Service.

retention.title: Data storage
retention.content: We will only retain your personal information for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which we collected it, including for the purposes of satisfying any legal, accounting or reporting requirements.When we no longer need your information, we will securely delete or anonymize it.

children.title: Data protection for children
children.content: Our service is not aimed at children under 13 years of age.We do not knowingly collect personal information from children under 13 years of age.If you are a parent or guardian and you are aware that your child has provided us with personal information, please contact us.

international.title: International data transfers
international.content: Your information may be transferred to and maintained on computers located outside of your state, province, country or other governmental jurisdiction where data protection laws may differ.By using our Service, you consent to the transfer of your information to our facilities and the third parties with whom we share it.

changes.title: Changes to this Privacy Policy
changes.intro: We may update our privacy policy from time to time.We will notify you of any changes by posting the new Privacy Policy on this page and updating the “Last Updated” date.
changes.effective: You are advised to periodically review this Privacy Policy for changes.Changes to this Privacy Policy will be effective when posted on this page.

acceptance.title: Acceptance of Terms

license.title: License of use
license.intro: Permission is granted to temporarily download one copy of the materials on sodusecure's website for personal, non-commercial transitory viewing.This is the grant of a license, not a transfer of title, and under this license you may not:

items.modify: Use the materials for any commercial purpose or public display

items.reverse: Remove any copyright or other proprietary notations from the materials

account.title: User accounts
account.content: When you create an account with us, you must provide accurate, complete, and current information.Failure to do so constitutes a breach of the Terms and may result in immediate termination of your account.

auditAi.title: Sodu /AuditAI – Subscription Terms
auditAi.intro: The following additional terms apply to the Sodu /AuditAI subscription (Starter, Studio, Pro+):

items.scope: Starter and Studio each cover one (1) repository. Pro+ covers up to four (4) repositories.
items.singleProject: On the Pro+ plan, all enrolled repositories must belong to the same product or project of the customer (e.g. frontend, backend, mobile app and infrastructure of the same product). Spreading multiple independent products or unrelated tenants across a single Pro+ subscription is not permitted.
items.verification: Sodu Secure may verify whether the enrolled repositories belong to the same project. If we have reasonable cause to suspect a violation, we may pause the affected audit runs and contact you for clarification; you have no right to a refund for delays resulting from such verification.
items.addOns: Additional repositories outside this scope (e.g. further products or further tenants) require a separate subscription or an individual quote.
items.setupFee: On the monthly subscription (Starter, Studio, Pro+) a one-time setup and onboarding fee of 500 € (plus VAT) is charged together with the first invoice. It covers repository onboarding, the baseline audit, Slack/Teams integration, and the onboarding call. On the annual subscription, setup and onboarding are already included and are not charged separately. The setup fee is non-refundable once onboarding has begun.
items.vat: All prices stated in these terms and on the checkout page are net prices and are understood plus the statutory value-added tax (19 % in Germany). Stripe shows the VAT separately on the invoice. For orders from other EU countries with a valid VAT ID, the reverse-charge procedure may apply.
items.billing: Billing takes place upfront at the selected interval (monthly or annual) via SEPA, credit card, or Apple/Google Pay through our payment processor Stripe. Statutory VAT is added on top of all amounts.
items.cancellation: Monthly subscriptions may be cancelled at any time effective at the end of the current billing month. Annual subscriptions may be cancelled effective at the end of the agreed 12-month term; without timely cancellation, the annual subscription renews for another 12 months. Payments already made are not refundable on a pro-rata basis.
items.access: We access enrolled repositories on a read-only basis only. Clones are fully deleted after each audit run.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.content: Your use of our Service is also governed by our Privacy Policy.Please read our Privacy Policy, which also governs the Site and informs users of our data collection practices.

prohibited.title: Prohibited Uses
prohibited.intro: You may only use the Service for lawful purposes and in accordance with the Terms.You agree not to use the Service:

items.violating: In a way that violates any applicable national or international law or regulation
items.framing: To transmit or send any advertising or promotional material without our prior written consent
items.uploading: To impersonate or attempt to impersonate the Company, a Company employee, another user, or any other person or entity
items.impersonate: In a way that violates the rights of others or is in any way illegal, threatening, fraudulent or harmful
items.harassing: To engage in any other conduct that restricts or inhibits anyone from using or enjoying the Service

intellectual.title: Intellectual property
intellectual.content: The Service and its original content, features and functionality are and will remain the exclusive property of sodusecure and its licensors.The Service is protected by copyright, trademark, and other laws.

limitation.title: Limitation of Liability
limitation.content: In no event shall sodusecure, nor its directors, employees, partners, agents, suppliers or affiliates, be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential or punitive damages, including without limitation, loss of profits, data, use, goodwill or other intangible losses.

termination.title: Termination
termination.content: We may terminate or suspend your account and bar access to the Service immediately, without prior notice or liability, under our sole discretion, for any reason whatsoever and without limitation, including but not limited to a breach of the Terms.

introduction.title: introduction
introduction.content: In the ever-evolving cybersecurity landscape, it is critical to stay ahead of emerging threats.This comprehensive guide examines the latest developments in vulnerabilities and provides actionable strategies for security teams.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: Modern applications face numerous security challenges.Understanding these vulnerabilities is the first step to building resilient systems.In this article, we examine the most critical security issues affecting applications today.

introduction.quote: Security is not a product, but a process.It's not just about tools, but about people, processes and technology working together to create a resilient security posture.

commonAttackVectors.title: Common attack vectors
items: SQL injection and database manipulation attacks
Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities in web applications
Insecure API endpoints and authentication management errors
Insecure direct object references that leak sensitive data
Security misconfigurations in cloud environments
Broken access control and authorization failures

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: The consequences of security breaches can be serious, from data leaks to full system compromise.Understanding these risks helps organizations effectively prioritize their security efforts and allocate resources appropriately.

averageCost.label: Average cost of a data breach

mitigationStrategies.title: Defense strategies
mitigationStrategies.content: Implementing effective security controls requires a layered approach.Here are proven strategies to strengthen your organization's security posture against common threats.

essentialPractices.title: Important security practices
items: Implement Defense in Depth with multiple layers of security
Conduct regular security assessments and penetration testing
Keep security policies and patches up to date
Enforce strong authentication and access controls
Implement comprehensive logging and monitoring
Provide ongoing security awareness training
Conduct regular code reviews and security audits
Implement proper input validation and output encoding

conclusion.title: Conclusion

react4shellDetail.title: React 4 Shell: RCE vulnerability and remediation strategies
react4shellDetail.description: A comprehensive security analysis of CVE-2024-50980 - understand the risks, identify the threat in your environment, and implement secure update paths.
react4shellDetail.author: Thomas Weber

introduction.title: introduction
introduction.content: React 4 Shell is a widely used frontend library used by thousands of developers worldwide.The recently discovered vulnerability CVE-2024-50980 allows Remote Code Execution (RCE) through manipulated input.In this article, we'll show you what this vulnerability means, how to identify it in your infrastructure, and how to fix it safely.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: CVE-2024-50980 is a critical remote code execution vulnerability in React 4 Shell < 1.8.4.Companies using this version should immediately upgrade to the patched version.This article shows you a safe migration path for production environments.

introduction.quote: Remote code execution is one of the most critical security flaws out there.A component used in most modern React applications makes this bug a priority for every developer.

commonAttackVectors.title: Technical details of the vulnerability
items: Insecure template rendering logic in React 4 Shell versions < 1.8.4
Lack of input validation when processing dynamic components
Insufficient output escaping in JSX generation
Exploitation through specially crafted URL parameters or POST data
Allows you to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the server context
Potential database access and privilege escalation

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: The impact of CVE-2024-50980 is far-reaching.An attacker could execute arbitrary code on the server, read files, compromise databases or inject malware.This is particularly critical for production environments that process sensitive customer data.

mitigationStrategies.title: Remediation and update strategies
mitigationStrategies.content: Eliminating this vulnerability requires a structured approach.Immediate measures should be combined with longer-term strategies.Here we show a proven update path for various scenarios.

essentialPractices.title: Step-by-step remediation plan
items: Step 1: Inventory all React 4 Shell installations in your infrastructure
Step 2: Update to React 4 Shell >= 1.8.4 in a test environment
Step 3: Perform comprehensive regression testing (UI, API, data flow)
Step 4: Plan a phased rollout in staging environment
Step 5: Monitor for anomalies during rollout
Step 6: Enable elevated logging and WAF rules during update
Step 7: Run post-update security tests
Step 8: Document the process and test results for compliance

conclusion.title: Conclusion and next steps
conclusion.content1: CVE-2024-50980 is a serious security vulnerability that requires immediate attention.Through a structured update process, you can minimize the risk and ensure the stability of your applications.
conclusion.content2: Sodu Secure offers professional penetration testing and security assessments to identify such critical vulnerabilities in your infrastructure before attackers discover them.Contact us for a comprehensive web application pentest or security audit of your React-based applications.

openClawDetail.title: OpenClaw Security Risks: Understanding AI Chatbot Vulnerabilities
openClawDetail.description: A detailed analysis of the security risks when integrating OpenClaw and other AI chatbots - prompt injection, data exfiltration and practical mitigation strategies.
openClawDetail.author: Julia Schmidt

introduction.title: Introduction to AI Security
introduction.content: OpenClaw and other Large Language Models (LLMs) offer revolutionary possibilities, but also raise new security risks.In this article, we examine the main threats associated with AI chatbot integration and show you how to mitigate them.

keyTakeaway.title: Critical insight
keyTakeaway.content: AI chatbots can be attacked through prompt injection – a new class of attacks specifically targeting LLMs.These attacks can leak sensitive data, execute unintended commands, or use the chatbot against your users.

introduction.quote: With the growing integration of AI into business-critical processes, companies must understand a new security paradigm.AI security isn’t just technical – it’s about trust, transparency and control.

commonAttackVectors.title: Common AI security risks
items: Prompt Injection Attacks – malicious input that causes the LLM to take undesirable actions
Data Exfiltration – Attackers attempt to trick the model into revealing sensitive training data
Jailbreaking – Techniques to circumvent security policies and guardrails
Model Poisoning – Attackers train models with malicious data
Denial of Service (DoS) – overloading AI systems with resource-intensive queries
Unauthorized API access – Insufficient authentication and rate limiting

impactAnalysis.title: Business impact
impactAnalysis.content: An AI security incident can have far-reaching consequences – from reputational damage to data loss to business interruptions.The dependence on AI systems is growing, so they need to be protected in the same way as traditional infrastructure.

identificationTime.label: Avg.Cost of an AI security incident

mitigationStrategies.title: Security strategies for AI integration
mitigationStrategies.content: There are proven techniques to make AI chatbots safer.A defense-in-depth strategy with multiple layers of security is required.

essentialPractices.title: Security measures for AI bots
items: Implement input validation and output filtering on all API endpoints
Use Content Security Policies (CSP) to limit prompt injection
Implement granular access control and API key management
Monitor queries for suspicious patterns and unusual usage
Set limits on token usage and query complexity
Implement logging and audit trails of all interactions
Conduct regular red team tests against your AI systems
Train your team on AI security best practices and common attack patterns

conclusion.title: AI security as a strategic priority
conclusion.content1: AI security is not optional – it is a business requirement.Companies that implement AI systems responsibly will have a competitive advantage.
conclusion.content2: Sodu Secure offers specialized penetration testing for AI systems and LLM integrations.We identify prompt injection vulnerabilities, API vulnerabilities and data protection risks before they are exploited by attackers.Contact us for a comprehensive AI security assessment.

smePentestDetail.title: Cybersecurity for SMEs: When you really need a pentest
smePentestDetail.description: A practical guide for small and medium-sized businesses - when penetration tests are necessary, what types there are and how they help your business.
smePentestDetail.author: Michael Hoffmann

introduction.title: Why SMEs need to take security seriously
introduction.content: Small and medium-sized businesses (SMEs) are often the target of cyberattacks.They often have fewer security resources than large corporations, but just as much to lose.This guide will show you how penetration testing can help your SME and when the time is right.

keyTakeaway.title: Key message
keyTakeaway.content: SMEs are the preferred targets of hackers - weaker defenses but with valuable data.A single successful attack can threaten the existence of an SME.A pentest is not a luxury, but a necessary business risk management tool.

introduction.quote: For SMBs, security isn't 'nice to have' - it's business continuity.A data leak can not only destroy your reputation, but also have legal consequences and you need to be aware of these risks.

commonAttackVectors.title: Most common attack vectors against SMEs
items: Phishing attacks and social engineering against employees
Weak passwords and lack of multi-factor authentication
Outdated software and missing security patches
Unsecured remote access systems and VPNs
Weak configurations in cloud systems (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)
Missing or unencrypted backups and data management

impactAnalysis.title: Financial and business impact
impactAnalysis.content: A cyber attack on an SME often has catastrophic consequences.Not just direct financial losses, but also business interruption, reputational damage and possibly bankruptcy.

averageCost.label: Avg.Costs of a cyber attack for SMEs

identificationTime.label: Mean time to detect an attack

multipleBreaches.label: SMEs without a formal security program

mitigationStrategies.title: When and how you should conduct a pentest
mitigationStrategies.content: Penetration testing is an important security measure, but it's important to understand when it provides the most value and which types are right for your SMB.

essentialPractices.title: Pentest strategy for SMEs
items: Time 1: Before your first major customer project or when security becomes critical
Time 2: After major system changes, upgrades or cloud migration
Time 3: At least annually if you process customer data
Time 4: After suspicious security events or malware infections
Choice of pentest type: Choose External Pentest for website/API (€5,000-15,000), Internal for network (€8,000-20,000)
Budget Tip: Start with a web application pentest as this is often the highest risk
Regularity: Annual security tests should be carried out after the first pentest
Measurability: Track how many findings are resolved and measure security progress

conclusion.title: Investment in security = investment in business continuity
conclusion.content1: For SMEs, a pentest is not optional – it is a necessary business management tool.A good understanding of your organization's security posture will help you make informed decisions and minimize risks.
conclusion.content2: Sodu Secure specializes in cost-effective but thorough penetration testing for SMEs.We understand the specific needs and budgets of smaller businesses and offer tailored solutions.From a simple web application security assessment to comprehensive network penetration tests – contact us for a non-binding consultation.

owaspTop10_2026Detail.title: OWASP Top 10 2026: New risks, new priorities
owaspTop10_2026Detail.description: A practical outlook on the OWASP Top 10 2026 - which trends are emerging and which measures are now a priority.
owaspTop10_2026Detail.author: Lisa Krueger

introduction.title: Classification 2026
introduction.content: The OWASP Top 10 is not a rigid checklist, but rather a reflection of current web risks.Clear trends are emerging for 2026: more complexity through microservices, more AI-supported attacks and increasing dependence on third-party providers.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: If you want to be resilient in 2026, you must combine security basics (access control, input validation, logging) with modern protective measures (supply chain protection, API security, secrets management).

introduction.quote: OWASP Top 10 does not mean checking off ten risks - but rather: managing ten priorities permanently.

commonAttackVectors.title: Risk trends that will dominate in 2026
items: Supply chain attacks through compromised dependencies
Flawed access control in distributed architectures
Insecure API endpoints and inadequate authentication
Insecure design and missing threat modeling processes
Misconfigurations in cloud and container environments
Weak secrets and key management in CI/CD pipelines

impactAnalysis.title: Why these risks are business critical
impactAnalysis.content: Damages range from data loss and business interruptions to regulatory penalties.The combination of automation and complexity is particularly dangerous: errors have a faster and broader impact than ever before.

identificationTime.label: Time to detection for supply chain incidents

multipleBreaches.label: Multiple systems affected by chain effects

mitigationStrategies.title: Practical measures for 2026
mitigationStrategies.content: Safety starts with design.For 2026, clear standards and regular tests that are based on modern attack paths are recommended.

essentialPractices.title: Prioritized security checks
items: Conduct regular web and API penetration testing
Establish mandatory threat modeling per feature
Use SBOMs and dependency scanning
Strengthen least privilege (MFA) access controls
Implement centralized logging and anomaly detection
Harden CI/CD with secrets scanning and signatures
Continuously test cloud configurations
Train teams in secure development practices

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: OWASP Top 10 2026 will be characterized by complexity, automation and supply chain risks.Companies that invest today will sustainably reduce their attack surface.
conclusion.content2: Sodu Secure supports you with web and API pen testing, threat modeling and security reviews.Talk to us to align your security strategy for 2026.

caseStudyDetail.title: Securing a FinTech web application
caseStudyDetail.description: Comprehensive security assessment uncovered critical vulnerabilities in payment processing workflows, preventing potential financial losses and ensuring regulatory compliance.

clientOverview.title: Customer overview
clientOverview.content: Our client is a leading financial company serving over 2 million users worldwide.As they prepared to scale their platform, they needed comprehensive security testing to ensure their infrastructure could withstand modern cyber threats while maintaining regulatory compliance and customer trust.

challenge.title: The challenge
challenge.intro: The organization faced several critical security concerns:
items: Legacy systems integrated with modern cloud infrastructure created complex attack surfaces
Fast development cycles meant that security testing was often given secondary consideration
Compliance requirements required thorough documentation and remediation
Previous automated scans had missed critical business logic vulnerabilities
Payment processing systems that process millions in daily transactions

phases.description: We began with thorough reconnaissance, mapping all assets, technologies and potential entry points.This included OSINT collection, subdomain enumeration and technology fingerprinting.
Manual testing combined with automated tools to identify vulnerabilities across all layers of the application stack, from infrastructure to business logic.
Securely exploit discovered vulnerabilities to validate impact and demonstrate real-world attack scenarios to stakeholders.
Detailed reports with executive summaries, technical results and remediation guides.Ongoing support throughout the remediation process.

keyFindings.title: Important results
keyFindings.summary: Our comprehensive assessment uncovered 47 vulnerabilities across multiple severities, with a particular focus on payment processing security.

critical.title: Critical vulnerabilities
items: SQL injection in the payment processing API allowing unauthorized database access
Broken authentication mechanism that allows account takeover
Insecure direct object references that expose sensitive financial information

high.title: High severity
items: Cross-site scripting (XSS) in the admin dashboard
Insecure file upload that allows remote code execution
Weak encryption of stored payment card data
Missing rate limiting on authentication endpoints
Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerabilities

medium.title: Medium severity
items: Information disclosure through error messages
Missing security headers and configurations
Deprecated third-party dependencies
Insufficient input validation
Weak password policies

low.title: Low severity
items: Minor Information Disclosures
Violations of best practices
Performance-related security issues
Using outdated protocols

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: The discovered vulnerabilities could have led to significant financial losses, regulatory non-compliance and damage to customer trust.Our assessment prevented potential breaches that would have cost millions in remediation costs and lost business.

remediation.title: Fix & Results
remediation.content: Working closely with the development team, we implemented comprehensive fixes for all critical and high-level vulnerabilities within the agreed time frame.

timeline.title: Remediation schedule
timeline.high: High level issues resolved within 1 week
timeline.medium: Medium priority fixes completed within 2 weeks
timeline.low: Low priority items addressed during regular maintenance

outcomes.title: Important results
items: All critical vulnerabilities eliminated before production deployment
Payment processing security significantly improved
Regulatory compliance requirements fully met
Development team trained in secure coding practices
Automated security testing integrated into CI/CD pipeline

oscpPathDetail.title: OSCP Continuation: Which Offensive Security Certification is Right for You?
oscpPathDetail.description: After the OSCP, the journey continues – OSEP, OSWE, OSED and more.A detailed guide to advanced offensive security certifications and how to take your penetration testing career to the next level.
oscpPathDetail.author: Sodu Secure Team

introduction.title: The OSCP journey doesn’t end – it’s just beginning
introduction.content: Congratulations – you have passed the OSCP (Offensive Security Certified Professional)!This legendary certification taught you how to attack real systems and shaped your mindset as a pentester.But many people ask themselves the question: What comes next?The good news: Offensive Security offers several advanced certifications that cover different specializations.

keyTakeaway.title: Important NOTE
keyTakeaway.content: Choosing the right post-OSCP certification depends on your career goal, interests, and technical strength.Each certification opens different doors - from advanced penetration testing to web application security to exploit development.

introduction.quote: After OSCP success, it's not about collecting more certificates - it's about delving into the areas that really excite you and that offer the most value to your career.

osepOverview.title: OSEP: Offensive Security Experienced Penetration Tester
osepOverview.subtitle: For advanced Red Team Operations and Evasion Techniques
osepOverview.content: The OSEP (formerly known as PEN-300) is the direct continuation of the OSCP and is aimed at pen testers who want to deepen their skills in realistic enterprise environments.Here you will learn how to use modern detection and prevention mechanisms.

topics.title: What you learn in OSEP:
items: Advanced Evasion Techniques against EDR/AV
Process Injection and In-Memory Execution
Active Directory Attacks (Kerberos, NTLM, Golden Tickets)
Lateral movement in segmented networks
C# and PowerShell for offensive tooling
Custom payload development
AppLocker and AMSI bypasses

examInfo.title: Exam details:
examInfo.duration: 48 hours of practical exam + 24 hours of documentation
examInfo.goal: Compromising a simulated enterprise environment with multiple network segments
examInfo.difficulty: Significantly more difficult than OSCP - requires deep understanding of Windows internals

osepOverview.bestFor: Ideal for: Red Teamers, Advanced Pentesters, Security Consultants who want to simulate enterprise attacks

osweOverview.title: OSWE: Offensive Security Web Expert
osweOverview.subtitle: For white-box web application security specialists
osweOverview.content: The OSWE (WEB-300) is perfect for those who want to specialize in Web Application Security.Unlike the OSCP, the focus here is on white box testing and source code review.

topics.title: What you learn in OSWE:
items: Manual code review of web applications (PHP, Java, .NET, Node.js)
Advanced SQLi Exploitation (also with WAFs)
Authentication bypass techniques
Deserialization exploits
Server Side Template Injection (SSTI)
Advanced XXE and SSRF
Custom exploit development for web apps
Bypassing web application firewalls

examInfo.title: Exam details:
examInfo.goal: Identification and exploitation of vulnerabilities in two web applications using source code
examInfo.difficulty: Requires very good programming skills and understanding of web frameworks

osweOverview.bestFor: Ideal for: Web App Penetration Testers, Bug Bounty Hunters, Application Security Engineers who perform in-depth code analysis

osedOverview.title: OSED: Offensive Security Exploit Developer
osedOverview.subtitle: For binary exploitation and exploit development experts
osedOverview.content: The OSED (EXP-301) is the most demanding of the post-OSCP certifications and focuses on Windows Exploit Development - a field that requires deep technical understanding.

topics.title: What you learn in OSED:
items: x86 assembly and debugging (WinDbg, Immunity Debugger)
Stack buffer overflows (SEH, DEP, ASLR bypasses)
Exploit development against modern Windows protections
Reverse engineering binaries
Format String Exploits
Egg Hunters and Custom Shellcode
ROP (Return Oriented Programming)
Heap Exploitation Basics

examInfo.title: Exam details:
examInfo.goal: Developed working exploits for multiple Windows binaries
examInfo.difficulty: Extremely demanding – requires deep understanding of processor architecture and memory management

osedOverview.bestFor: Ideal for: Exploit Developers, Vulnerability Researchers, Malware Analysts who want to get into binary exploitation

otherCerts.title: Additional offensive security certifications
otherCerts.content: In addition to OSEP, OSWE and OSED, there are other specialized offensive security certifications:

certifications.name: OSWP (Offensive Security Wireless Professional)
certifications.description: Focus on wireless network penetration testing – WLAN security, encryption attacks, rogue access points

certifications.name: OSDA (Offensive Security Defense Analyst)
certifications.description: The defensive perspective – SOC Analysis, Threat Hunting, SIEM, Incident Response

certifications.name: OSMR (Offensive Security macOS Researcher)
certifications.description: Specialization in macOS Security – macOS Internals, Privilege Escalation, Objective-C Exploitation

certifications.name: OSEE (Offensive Security Exploitation Expert)
certifications.description: The ultimate challenge – advanced exploitation techniques, kernel exploitation, zero-day research

decisionGuide.title: Decision support: Which certification is right for you?
decisionGuide.content: The choice depends on several factors:

scenarios.profile: You want to conduct red team operations
scenarios.recommendation: OSEP is the best choice.You will learn realistic attack techniques against enterprise environments.

scenarios.profile: You love web applications and programming
scenarios.recommendation: OSWE is perfect.You will become a web application security expert with a white box focus.

scenarios.profile: You are interested in low-level programming
scenarios.recommendation: OSED opens the door to the world of binary exploitation and exploit development.

scenarios.profile: You want to build broad knowledge
scenarios.recommendation: Start with OSEP (generalist), then specialize with OSWE or OSED.

scenarios.profile: You work in the bug bounty area
scenarios.recommendation: OSWE helps you find complex web vulnerabilities that others miss.

preparation.title: Preparation and tips
preparation.content: Each of these certifications is demanding and requires intensive preparation:

tips.title: Use lab time
tips.description: All courses come with extensive lab environments.Make the most of every minute – practice makes the difference.

tips.title: Documentation from the start
tips.description: Get in the habit of documenting every step.This saves you valuable time during the exam.

tips.title: Use community
tips.description: The OffSec Discord and Reddit (r/oscp, r/AskNetsec) are valuable resources for questions and motivation.

tips.title: Schedule breaks
tips.description: For 48-hour exams, break management is crucial.Test your rhythm in the lab.

tips.title: Build prior knowledge
tips.description: For OSWE: Consolidate your programming skills.For OSED: Learn assembly basics in advance.

commonAttackVectors.title: Common challenges in OffSec exams
items: Active Directory Exploitation – Kerberoasting, Pass-the-Hash, DCSync Attacks
Web Application Exploitation – SQLi, XSS, Authentication Bypasses, Deserialization
Buffer Overflow Development – ​​Stack-based exploits with DEP/ASLR bypasses
Privilege Escalation – Linux and Windows post-exploitation techniques
Network Pivoting – Lateral Movement through Segmented Networks
Custom Payload Development – ​​Bypassing AV/EDR with C#, PowerShell and Shellcode

impactAnalysis.title: Career Impact: Why these certifications are important
impactAnalysis.content: Offensive security certifications are among the most respected in the cybersecurity industry.They not only demonstrate theoretical knowledge, but also practical skills in realistic attack scenarios.

averageCost.label: Average salary increase according to OSCP/OSEP

multipleBreaches.label: Success rate on first attempt after complete lab implementation

mitigationStrategies.title: Success strategies for OffSec certifications
mitigationStrategies.content: The key to success lies not only in technical knowledge, but also in the right approach and preparation.

essentialPractices.title: Best practices
items: Try Harder Mindset – Perseverance is often more important than talent
Enumerate, Enumerate, Enumerate – Thorough information gathering is the basis of every successful attack
Document every step - screenshots and notes from the very first moment
Use the lab intensively – plan at least 60-90 days of active lab time
Use community resources – study groups, Discord, Reddit and OffSec forums
Break management – ​​For 48-hour exams: plan 6-8 hours of sleep
Backup strategies – Always plan B and C for stuck situations
Build an exploit database – maintain your own exploit library and cheat sheets

conclusion.title: Conclusion: Your path after the OSCP
conclusion.content1: The OSCP is just the beginning of your Offensive Security journey.Each of the featured certifications offers unique skills and career opportunities.OSEP deepens your red team skills, OSWE turns you into a web security expert, and OSED opens the door to exploit development.
conclusion.content2: The important thing is to choose the certification that fits your interests and serves your career goals.Quality takes precedence over quantity - it's better to master one certification properly than to go through several superficially.
conclusion.finalThought: And remember: these certifications are hard, but that's what makes them valuable.The Offensive Security Labs and Exams force you to truly understand, not just memorize.This is why OffSec certificates are so highly regarded in the industry.

sqlInjectionDetail.title: Understanding SQL Injection: Deep Dive into Database Security
sqlInjectionDetail.description: SQL injection remains one of the most dangerous web vulnerabilities.Learn about attack patterns, risks and practical defense strategies.
sqlInjectionDetail.author: Dr.Sarah Mitchell

introduction.title: Why SQL injection is still a top risk
introduction.content: Despite modern frameworks, SQL injection attacks remain common.Even small input errors can lead to attackers reading out data, manipulating it or compromising entire systems.In this article we show how SQLi is created, how attacks work and which measures really protect.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: SQLi is rarely a single error - it is usually a chain of missing input validation, unsafe query building and database rights that are too broad.

introduction.quote: When databases are open, any application is only as secure as its weakest query.

commonAttackVectors.title: Common attack patterns
items: Unsafe string concatenation in SQL queries
Lack of parameterization in login and search functions
Error-Based SQLi through detailed error messages
Blind SQLi via time-based queries
Second-order SQLi through saved user input
ORM misconfigurations with direct query access

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: SQL injection can lead to data leakage, customer data manipulation, and complete system takeover.Personal data is particularly affected – with immediate GDPR risks.

averageCost.label: Ø Cost of a data leak

multipleBreaches.label: Web apps with critical input errors

mitigationStrategies.title: Defense strategies
mitigationStrategies.content: Effective protection comes from technical controls and regular tests.

essentialPractices.title: Proven measures
items: Only use prepared statements and parameter binding
Establish central input validation and output encoding
Configure database accounts according to least privilege
Enable WAF rules against typical SQLi payloads
Minimize error messages in production
Integrate SAST/DAST into CI/CD
Regular code reviews with a security focus
Carry out pen tests including retests

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: SQL injection is old but highly effective.Whoever protects databases protects the entire company.
conclusion.content2: A structured pentest reliably uncovers SQLi and provides clear remediation measures - ideal for security and compliance.

zeroDayDetail.title: Zero-day vulnerabilities: detect, respond, secure
zeroDayDetail.description: Zero days are the most dangerous vulnerabilities.Learn how to detect attacks early, test response plans and reduce risks.
zeroDayDetail.author: Marcus Chen

introduction.title: If there is no patch
introduction.content: Zero-day vulnerabilities are particularly critical because they can be exploited before a patch exists.For companies this means: detection, response and resilience are crucial.We show which early indicators help and which controls are effective.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: Zero-day risks can never be completely eliminated - but they can be massively reduced through monitoring, segmentation and incident response.

introduction.quote: With zero days, it's not the one with the most tools that wins, but the one with the quickest reactions.

commonAttackVectors.title: Typical attack routes
items: Spearphishing with exploit attachment
Drive-by downloads via compromised websites
Supply chain attacks on software updates
Exploits in VPN and remote access systems
Privilege escalation after initial access
Lateral motion in shallow networks

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: Zero-day attacks often result in complete system compromise.Without early warning, attackers often remain undetected for a long time.

averageCost.label: Ø Length of stay of undetected attackers

identificationTime.label: Target time for incident response start

mitigationStrategies.title: Defense strategies
mitigationStrategies.content: Zero-day resilience comes from a combination of prevention and response.

essentialPractices.title: Proven measures
items: EDR/threat detection with high-fidelity alerts
Network segmentation and strict access limits
Threat intelligence feeds for indicators
Accelerate security patching (including out-of-band)
Hardening of critical systems (VPN, email, IAM)
Test incident response plans regularly
Verify backups and recovery plans
Red teaming and pen testing for simulation

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: Zero days are inevitable – being unprepared is not.
conclusion.content2: A tested response plan and regular security testing dramatically reduce the impact.

apiSecurityDetail.title: API Security: Best Practices for Modern Applications
apiSecurityDetail.description: APIs are the backbone of digital products.Learn which risks are most common and how to make your interfaces secure.
apiSecurityDetail.author: Elena Rodríguez

introduction.title: Why APIs are so often the gateway
introduction.content: APIs connect systems, partners and apps.That's exactly why they are an attractive target.Misconfigurations, weak authentication or insufficient validation often lead to critical data leaks.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: API security starts with clear permission models and ends with continuous testing - ideally in every release pipeline.

introduction.quote: If you don't test your APIs, you're testing in production - by attackers.

commonAttackVectors.title: Common API attack vectors
items: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
Weak authentication and session handling
Missing rate limits and abuse of endpoints
Mass assignment and excessive data exposure
Injection into query parameters and filters
Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: API gaps often lead directly to data leakage because APIs provide structured data.The impact is particularly high for personal data sets.

averageCost.label: Proportion of apps with API auth vulnerabilities

identificationTime.label: Ø Time until API errors are discovered

multipleBreaches.label: Most common OWASP API top 10 vector

mitigationStrategies.title: Defense strategies
mitigationStrategies.content: API security requires technical controls and clear governance.

essentialPractices.title: Proven measures
items: Use OAuth2/OIDC with short-lived tokens
Check access rights per object (avoid BOLA)
Schema validation for requests/responses
Activate rate limits and abuse protection
Securely manage secrets and API keys
Logging and monitoring per endpoint
Automate security tests in CI/CD
Regular API penetration testing

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: APIs are business critical – and need the same protection as your core systems.
conclusion.content2: A dedicated API pentest uncovers auth and permission errors before they are exploited.

introduction.quote: A secure app not only protects the device - but the entire data chain behind it.

Client-side trust without server validation

Server-side validation of all critical actions
Obfuscation and anti-tampering measures

Regular updates for SDKs and libraries
Mobile Threat Modeling according to MASVS
Regular mobile app pentests

Lack of network segmentation
Open management ports and admin interfaces
Exposed secrets in CI/CD
Insecure container and Kubernetes defaults

Enforce security baselines via IaC
CSPM tools for continuous compliance
Encryption at rest and in transit everywhere
Centralized logging and SIEM integration
Network segmentation and zero trust models
Secrets management for CI/CD
Regular cloud penetration testing

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: Cloud is secure – if configuration, rights and monitoring are correct.
conclusion.content2: A cloud pentest reveals misconfigurations and security vulnerabilities before they are exploited.

keyTakeaway.content: Security awareness is not an annual training course - but a continuous process with measurable exercises.

items: Spearphishing with deceptively real senders
Vishing (telephone fraud) with pressure situations
Smishing (SMS/Chat) with malicious links
Pretexting and impersonation

Phishing simulations with measurable KPIs
Strong MFA and Conditional Access

Least Privilege and Credential Hygiene

gdprPentestDetail.title: GDPR & Penetration Testing: How the two are related and why it is crucial
gdprPentestDetail.description: GDPR requires appropriate technical measures - penetration tests provide proof.Find out how pentests support Art. 32, reduce risks and make data protection tangible.
gdprPentestDetail.author: Sodu Secure Team

introduction.title: Why GDPR doesn’t work without security
introduction.content: The GDPR is not just a documentation topic.In Article 32 it calls for “appropriate technical and organizational measures”.This is exactly where penetration tests provide measurable security: They show whether systems are really resilient - and not just on paper.This article explains how GDPR and pentesting fit together and why both together are the most effective protection for personal data.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: Penetration testing is not an explicit GDPR requirement, but it is the strongest evidence that your security measures are effective.Anyone who verifiably checks risks significantly reduces the risk of fines and liability.

introduction.quote: Data protection without technical security is just a promise.A pen test makes this promise verifiable.

commonAttackVectors.title: Where GDPR risks typically arise
items: Missing or overly broad access controls (RBAC)
Insecure APIs with insufficient authentication and authorization
Unencrypted personal data in databases or backups
Misconfigurations in cloud environments (publicly accessible buckets)
Weak session management and poor multi-factor implementation
Inadequate logging and monitoring of data access

impactAnalysis.title: Impact analysis
impactAnalysis.content: A data breach is not just a technical issue.It leads to reporting requirements, potential fines and an often irreversible loss of trust.Penetration tests help to make these risks visible at an early stage and demonstrably reduce them.

identificationTime.label: Obligation to report after becoming known

mitigationStrategies.title: GDPR-compliant security strategy with pentests
mitigationStrategies.content: The most effective path to GDPR compliance is a risk-based security approach.Penetration testing complements technical controls, provides evidence and prioritizes actions.

essentialPractices.title: Best practices
items: Data classification and data flow analysis (identify personal data)
Scope definition for pentests based on Art. 32 and risk analysis
Regular web, API and infrastructure pentests with post-testing
Verify strong encryption for data in transit and at rest
Technically validate least privilege models and role rights
Prioritize, resolve, and document vulnerabilities with SLAs
Check incident response processes and logging at GDPR level
Provide auditable reports as proof of compliance

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.content1: GDPR and penetration tests belong together: The regulation not only requires policies, but also effective technical measures.A pen test shows whether these measures work in practice.
conclusion.content2: With a structured test plan you get clear priorities, measurable improvements and reliable evidence of data protection compliance.Sodu Secure supports you with tailor-made GDPR-oriented pentests.

kmuSicherheitslueckenDetail.title: 5 security gaps that we find in almost every SME
kmuSicherheitslueckenDetail.description: Cyber ​​attacks no longer only affect corporations.Medium-sized companies are particularly attractive because they have valuable data but often do not perform full security validation.
kmuSicherheitslueckenDetail.author: Kerim K.

introduction.title: Why SMEs are particularly at risk
introduction.content: Cyber ​​attacks no longer only affect corporations.Medium-sized companies are particularly attractive because they have valuable data but often do not perform full security validation.In our Berlin pilot program and in regular pentests, we encounter the same critical vulnerabilities again and again.This article summarizes the five most common ones – with concrete insights from practice.

keyTakeaway.title: Important insight
keyTakeaway.content: Security does not start with a tool - but with a realistic assessment of your own attack surface.If you know your weak points, you can reduce risks in a targeted manner – before it becomes expensive.

introduction.quote: An attacker only needs one way in.You have to protect everyone.

commonAttackVectors.title: The 5 most common security gaps in SMBs
items: 1. Unprotected or misconfigured Active Directory structures – overprivileged accounts, lack of segmentation, legacy protocols
2. Publicly accessible systems with known vulnerabilities – outdated VPN gateways, open management ports, insecure SSL configurations
3. Weak email security & missing DMARC configuration - no SPF/DKIM, DMARC set to 'none', lack of anti-spoofing protection
4. Web applications without in-depth security testing – lack of access controls, IDOR vulnerabilities, weak authentication
5. No tested backup and restore strategy – backups are never restored, no offline backups, unclear restart times

impactAnalysis.title: Why these gaps are so critical
impactAnalysis.content: Each of these five vulnerabilities can have catastrophic consequences on their own.When combined – and attackers routinely combine them – they create attack chains that can go from initial phishing to full ransomware encryption in just a few hours.

averageCost.label: Average damage per ransomware attack among SMBs

identificationTime.label: Average time an attacker stays undetected

multipleBreaches.label: of AD environments have critical misconfigurations

mitigationStrategies.title: What you can do specifically
mitigationStrategies.content: The good news: All five vulnerabilities are remediable.The first step is always a realistic assessment - a professional pen test shows where you are actually vulnerable, not where you think you are.

essentialPractices.title: Immediate measures for SMEs
items: Active Directory: Perform rights audit, deactivate legacy protocols (NTLMv1, SMBv1).
External attack surface: Inventory open ports, keep VPN software up to date
Email security: Configure SPF, DKIM and DMARC correctly and set to 'reject'
Web applications: Have access controls and authentication checked regularly
Backups: Carry out a recovery test at least once a year, create an offline copy
Awareness: Conduct regular phishing simulations for employees
Pentest: Commission an external penetration test at least once a year

conclusion.title: Our pilot program – get tested now for free
conclusion.content1: As part of our market launch, we are currently offering selected Berlin SMEs a complete pen test free of charge - including Active Directory analysis, phishing simulation and management report.
conclusion.content2: Only 2 of 7 spots available.Apply now at sodusecure.com/berlin-kmu-pilot and find out where your attack surface really lies - before an attacker does.

pentestAblaufDetail.title: Pentest process: How does a penetration test really work?(2026)
pentestAblaufDetail.description: Explained step by step: How does a professional penetration test work?From order clarification to the active test phase to the finished report – the complete pentest process.
pentestAblaufDetail.author: Kerim K.

introduction.title: What is a penetration test – and why is the process crucial?
introduction.content: A penetration test – pentest for short – is an authorized, simulated attack on your IT infrastructure.The goal is to find vulnerabilities before a real attacker does.But it's not the use of tools that makes a good pentest - it's the structured process.Anyone who knows the phases, knows what they are getting, can evaluate the provider and understands the report.This article explains the complete pentest process step by step.

keyTakeaway.title: The most important thing first
keyTakeaway.content: A professional pentest follows a defined process in several phases - from the clarification of the order to active exploitation to the final presentation.Each phase delivers specific results and builds on the previous one.

introduction.quote: A pentest without a clear process is not a test - it is an experiment at the expense of your infrastructure.

commonAttackVectors.title: The 8 phases of the penetration testing process
items: Phase 1 – Order clarification & scope definition: Joint definition of test limits, target systems, contact persons and time windows.Everything is written down in the pentest contract.
Phase 2 - Reconnaissance: Passive and active information collection about the target - OSINT, DNS analysis, subdomains, public leaks, employee data.
Phase 3 – Scanning & Enumeration: Systematic recording of open ports, services, operating systems and software versions with manual verification of each finding.
Phase 4 - Vulnerability Analysis: Identification of exploitable vulnerabilities - CVEs, misconfigurations, business logic flaws, weak authentication.
Phase 5 - Exploitation (Active Exploitation): Manual attempt to exploit identified vulnerabilities and prove that they are actually vulnerable - no false positive reporting.
Phase 6 – Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement: After initial access: privilege escalation, network pivoting, domain admin attempt, persistence – how far does a real attacker get?
Phase 7 – Reporting & Documentation: Technical report with CVSS assessment, executive summary, proof of concept screenshots and prioritized remediation roadmap.
Phase 8 – Final presentation & retest: Presentation of the results to IT and management.After the problem has been resolved, we recommend a free retest of the critical findings.

impactAnalysis.title: How long does a penetration test take?
impactAnalysis.content: The duration of a pen test depends heavily on the scope.The following time frames apply as a rough guide - the active test phase is only part of the overall effort.

statistics.title: Typical turnaround times by pentest type

averageCost.label: Focused web application pentest (1 application, defined scope)

identificationTime.label: External pentest (network + web + cloud, SME size)

multipleBreaches.label: Complete SMB Pentest (External + Active Directory + Phishing)

mitigationStrategies.title: Preparation: What you should do before the pentest
mitigationStrategies.content: A well-prepared company gets more out of the pentest.This checklist will help you design the process optimally – and send your infrastructure well-positioned for the test.

essentialPractices.title: Pentest preparation: checklist for companies
items: Written authorization: Pentest contract with explicit authorization – legally protects both sides.
Name contact person: Specify the person responsible for IT and emergency contact for the test period.
Define scope in writing: Document IP ranges, domains, applications and explicit exclusions.
Coordinate the test period: Choose times with few disruptions – protect production systems if possible.
Ensure complete backups: Completely back up all target systems before testing begins.
Maximize logging: Increase SIEM/logging depth for the test phase - valuable learning material.
Have an incident response plan ready: In the unlikely event of real disruptions during testing.
Schedule a retest: Reserve time and budget for reverification of the corrected findings.

conclusion.title: Order a pen test: What to do now
conclusion.content1: If you understand the process of a penetration test, you can select the right provider, set realistic expectations and prepare the test optimally.SODU Secure accompanies you through all 8 phases – transparent, structured and with comprehensible results.
conclusion.content2: Berlin SMEs can still apply for our free pilot program 2026: full pentest worth up to €15,000 free of charge.Only 2 places available.Apply now at sodusecure.com/berlin-kmu-pilot.

pentestBerlinDetail.description: How much does a pen test cost in Berlin?What types are there?When does a Berlin company need a penetration test – and how do you find the right provider?The complete guide.
pentestBerlinDetail.author: Kerim K.

introduction.title: Why Berlin companies need a pen test now
introduction.content: Berlin is Germany's leading technology and startup location - and at the same time one of the most attractive targets for cybercriminals.Companies of all sizes process sensitive customer data, operate critical IT infrastructure and are connected to the world via the cloud.At the same time, many Berlin SMEs and medium-sized companies have not carried out a complete security validation of their own systems.A penetration test – pentest for short – closes exactly this gap: It shows where your infrastructure is really vulnerable before a real attacker does.

keyTakeaway.title: The most important thing in brief
keyTakeaway.content: A pen test in Berlin is not a luxury measure for corporations - it is a realistic security tool for any company that operates digital assets, customer data or critical IT processes.The question is not if you should be tested, but when.

introduction.quote: An attacker only needs an open door.SODU Secure shows you where these doors are - before anyone else does.

commonAttackVectors.title: What is tested in a pen test in Berlin
items: External attack surface: Public IPs, domains, cloud services, VPN gateways, exposed ports and services - anything an attacker can reach from the Internet.
Web applications: OWASP Top 10, Business Logic Flaws, Authentication Bypasses, IDOR, XXE, SSRF - manual and in-depth analysis of your web apps and APIs.
Active Directory & Internal Infrastructure: Kerberoasting, Pass-the-Hash, AS-REP Roasting, GPO exploits, lateral movement – ​​how far can an attacker get once they are in the network?
Phishing simulation: Realistic email campaigns based on the latest technology – with click rate evaluation and awareness analysis of your employees.
Cloud Security: Azure, AWS, GCP – misconfigurations, overprivileged identities, insecure storage buckets, and cloud-native attack paths.
Mobile & API: Mobile apps (iOS/Android) and backend APIs – authentication, authorization, data leaks, unencrypted transmission.

impactAnalysis.title: Pentest Berlin: costs, duration and process
impactAnalysis.content: One of the most common reasons why Berlin companies do not commission a pen test is uncertainty about costs and effort.A well-structured pen test is more predictable than many people think.

statistics.title: Typical costs for a pentest in Berlin

averageCost.label: Focused web application pentest (1 app, 3-5 days testing)

multipleBreaches.label: Complete SME pentest (External + AD + Phishing + Report)

mitigationStrategies.title: How to find the right pentest provider in Berlin
mitigationStrategies.content: There are a growing number of IT security companies in Berlin - but not all of them offer real manual pentesting.Here are the most important selection criteria:

essentialPractices.title: Checklist: The right pentest partner in Berlin
items: Manual testing instead of pure scan reports: Ask explicitly whether your tester develops exploits manually or only uses automated tools.
Clear scope & fixed price: A reputable provider defines the scope in writing and gives a fixed price - no hourly rate surprises.
Full documentation: The report should include executive summary, technical details, CVSS scores and prioritized remediation roadmap.
Contractual protection: Pentest contract with authorization, disclaimer and data protection regulation – protects both sides.
Retest included: Good providers offer a free retest of the critical findings after the problem has been resolved.
Transparency about methodology: PTES, OWASP Testing Guide, MITER ATT&CK – reputable pen testers work according to established frameworks.
Local accessibility: A Berlin provider with an on-site option is a real advantage, especially for final presentations and emergency responses.
References & Transparency: Reputable Berlin pentest providers name reference projects or provide anonymized reports upon request - ask actively about them.

conclusion.content1: SODU Secure is your pentest partner in Berlin.We offer manual testing, transparent fixed prices and reports that people actually read - not a 200-page PDF full of false positives.

itSecurityCheckFirmaDetail.title: IT Security Check Company: What is an IT security check for companies?(2026)
itSecurityCheckFirmaDetail.description: What does an IT security check for companies include?Which areas are checked, how much does it cost and when does your company need an IT security check?The complete practical guide from SODU Secure Berlin.
itSecurityCheckFirmaDetail.author: Kerim K.

introduction.title: What is an IT security check for companies?
introduction.content: An IT security check - also called an IT security check or IT security audit - is a systematic check of a company's IT infrastructure, systems and processes for security gaps, misconfigurations and vulnerabilities.Unlike pure vulnerability scanning or an automated compliance audit, a professional IT security check combines manual testing methods with automated tools - and thus provides a realistic picture of the actual attack surface.For companies of all sizes - from 20-person SMEs to medium-sized companies - a regular IT security check is no longer an option, but a requirement.This guide explains what exactly is checked, how much an IT security check costs and how you can optimally prepare your company.

keyTakeaway.title: The most important thing in brief
keyTakeaway.content: An IT security check for companies is not a one-time measure, but rather a continuous process.Anyone who knows their vulnerabilities can take targeted and cost-effective countermeasures - before an attacker finds the gaps.

introduction.quote: If you don't know where you are vulnerable, you can't protect yourself.An IT security check creates clarity.

commonAttackVectors.title: What an IT security check for companies includes
items: External attack surface: Publicly accessible services, IP addresses, domains, cloud services, VPN gateways and exposed ports - anything an attacker can see from the Internet without credentials.

Internal network security & Active Directory: Misconfigurations in Windows domains, overprivileged accounts, insecure group policies, legacy protocols (NTLMv1, SMBv1) and lateral movement possibilities in the network.

Web Applications & APIs: Authentication weaknesses, lack of access controls, injection attacks (SQL, LDAP, Command Injection), IDOR and other OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities in corporate or public web apps.
Email Security & Phishing Vulnerability: Missing or misconfigured SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records;Phishing simulation to assess employee awareness.
Cloud security (Azure, Microsoft 365, AWS): Misconfigurations in cloud services, overprivileged service accounts, insecure storage configurations and weak multi-factor authentication.
Endpoint Security & Patch Status: Unpatched operating systems and applications, missing EDR solutions, insecure local administrator configurations.
Backup & Disaster Recovery: Are backups created regularly and – crucially – actually restored and tested?Are there offline copies?
Organizational measures: IT security guidelines, access management, onboarding/offboarding processes, employee training status.

impactAnalysis.title: Why every company needs an IT security check
impactAnalysis.content: The threat situation has changed dramatically in recent years.Ransomware, phishing and targeted attacks on SMEs have become the norm - no longer just for corporations.A professional IT security check shows you specifically where your company stands today.

averageCost.label: of all cyber attacks worldwide target small and medium-sized businesses (Verizon DBIR)

identificationTime.label: Average time an attacker stays in the network before being discovered (IBM)

multipleBreaches.label: Average damage caused by a ransomware attack on SMEs – without any follow-up costs

mitigationStrategies.title: IT Security Check Company: How much does it cost and how does it work?
mitigationStrategies.content: The costs for an IT security check depend on the scope, size of the IT infrastructure and the depth of the check.Here is a realistic overview for small and medium-sized companies in Germany:

essentialPractices.title: Typical price ranges for an IT security check
items: Basic IT security check (automated scan + short report): €500 – €1,500 – suitable for very small companies as an initial overview, but not a replacement for manual testing.
Professional IT security check for SMEs (manual + automated, external attack surface): €2,000 – €5,000 – realistic picture of the external security situation.
Complete IT security check including internal test & AD: €5,000 – €12,000 – includes external + internal audit, Active Directory analysis, email security and management report.
IT security check including phishing simulation: €7,000 – €15,000 – complete SME security package with employee awareness test.
Process: Kick-off and scope definition → Reconnaissance and OSINT → Active tests (external, internal, phishing) → Report creation → Final presentation → Implement recommendations.

itSecurityAuditDetail.title: IT Security Audit & IS Security Audit: The Complete Guide for Businesses (2026)
itSecurityAuditDetail.description: What is an IT security audit?What is the difference to the IS security audit?How does an IT security audit work, how much does it cost – and how can your company optimally prepare?Everything in one practical guide from SODU Secure.
itSecurityAuditDetail.author: Kerim K.

introduction.title: IT Security Audit vs. IS Security Audit: What is the difference?
introduction.content: In the German corporate environment, the terms IT security audit, IT security audit and IS security audit are often used interchangeably - but they have different focuses.An IT security audit checks specific technical security measures: systems, networks, access rights, patch status and configurations.It is technology-centric and is often the first step to a more comprehensive security program.An IS security audit (information security audit) is broader: It checks not only technology, but also processes, guidelines, organizational structures and compliance with standards such as ISO 27001, BSI IT-Grundschutz or NIS2.Both formats are relevant for companies in Germany - and complement each other ideally with a penetration test that practically proves the effectiveness of the measures found.

keyTakeaway.title: Important distinction
keyTakeaway.content: IT Security Audit = technical examination of systems and configurations.IS security audit = holistic review including processes, guidelines, compliance.Pentest = practical attack simulation to validate both levels.

introduction.quote: An IT security audit shows whether your measures exist.A penetration test shows whether they actually work.

commonAttackVectors.title: What an IT security audit checks for companies
items: Network Security & Segmentation: Firewall rules, VLAN configurations, DMZ construction, inter-VLAN routing risks, unauthorized communication paths between network segments.
Identity & Access Management (IAM): role concepts, least privilege principle, privileged access management, stale accounts (inactive accounts with active rights), service account security.
Active Directory & Windows infrastructure: configuration of domains, group policies (GPOs), Kerberos settings, LAPS deployment, delegation of permissions.
Patch management & vulnerability status: Up-to-dateness of operating systems, software and firmware used;evaluation of open CVEs;Patch cycles and priorities.
Cloud security configuration: Azure AD / Entra ID, Microsoft 365, AWS or GCP - Security Score, Conditional Access Policies, MFA enforcement, data protection settings.
Backup & Business Continuity: Backup strategy (3-2-1 rule), encryption of backups, recovery tests (RTO/RPO), ransomware resistance of the backup infrastructure.
Policies & Organizational Controls: Existence and timeliness of IT security policies, password policies, acceptable use policies, incident response processes.
Compliance check (NIS2, GDPR/DSGVO Art. 32, ISO 27001, BSI IT-Grundschutz): Assessment of the technical and organizational measures (TOMs) and the documentation obligations.

impactAnalysis.title: When does a company need an IT security audit?
impactAnalysis.content: There are clear situations in which an IT security audit is not only useful but urgently necessary.At the same time, security experts recommend establishing an IT security audit as a regular measure – similar to an annual tax audit.

statistics.title: Typical triggers for an IT security audit

averageCost.label: Regulatory obligation: NIS2 directive, GDPR Art. 32 and BSI IT-Grundschutz require regular security checks

identificationTime.label: After a security incident: Forensics, damage assessment and hardening plan require a systematic audit

multipleBreaches.label: During company changes: New locations, takeovers or system migrations increase the attack surface

mitigationStrategies.title: Process and costs of an IT security audit in Germany
mitigationStrategies.content: A professional IT security audit follows a clear process.The duration and costs depend on the scope, the size of the company and the level of detail.Here is a realistic overview:

essentialPractices.title: Phases of an IT security audit
items: Phase 1 – Scope & Kick-off: Definition of the scope of the test (in-scope / out-of-scope systems), coordination of the methodology (technical, organizational or both), scheduling.
Phase 2 - Intelligence Collection & OSINT: Passive reconnaissance, DNS and IP analysis, overview of the external attack surface without active testing.
Phase 3 – Technical review (audit): Manual and automated analysis of the in-scope systems – networks, endpoints, AD, cloud, web apps, APIs.
Phase 4 – Organizational review: Interviews with IT managers, review of guidelines, access concepts, backup documentation and incident response plans.
Phase 5 – Findings analysis & report: Consolidation of all findings, CVSS assessment, prioritization according to risk, creation of the audit report (executive summary + technical part).
Phase 6 – Final presentation: presentation of the results to management and IT;prioritized roadmap for the implementation of measures.
Cost orientation for SMEs (20–150 employees): basic audit (technical) €3,000–7,000;complete IS security audit (technical + organizational) €8,000–20,000;combined with pentest from €10,000.
